Notes: Data_Visualization- Data Visualization using Tableau

Data Visualization using Tableau
​​​​​​​Tableau provides a powerful platform for creating interactive and visually appealing data visualizations.

Tableau Documentation: Build a Basic View to Explore Your Data

A step-by-step guide to performing data visualization using Tableau:

1. Connect to Data:
Launch Tableau and connect to your data source. Tableau supports various data sources such as databases, spreadsheets, and web services. You can connect to the data by selecting the appropriate connection option and specifying the necessary credentials or file location.



​

2. Explore the Data:
Once connected, Tableau will display a data preview. Take some time to explore the data structure, review the available fields, and understand the relationships between different data elements. This will help you plan your visualizations effectively.


​
3. Choose a Visualization Type:
Tableau offers a wide range of visualization types, including bar charts, line charts, scatter plots, maps, and more. Select the appropriate visualization type that best represents your data and insights. You can choose the visualization type from the "Show Me" pane or use the drag-and-drop interface to add fields to the view and let Tableau suggest suitable visualizations.

​
4. Build Visualization:
Drag and drop the desired dimensions and measures onto the workspace. Dimensions represent categorical data such as names, dates, or regions, while measures represent quantitative data like sales figures or quantities. Tableau will automatically create the visualization based on the selected fields. You can further refine the visualization by applying filters, sorting data, or adding additional dimensions or measures.

​
5. Enhance the Visualization:
Customize the visualization to make it more informative and visually appealing. Tableau provides various options to format and enhance visualizations. You can adjust color schemes, fonts, and axis labels. You can also add titles, annotations, tooltips, and reference lines to provide context and insights.

6. Create Interactivity:
Tableau allows users to create interactive visualizations by adding filters, parameters, and actions. Filters enable users to interactively explore different subsets of data. Parameters allow users to change certain aspects of the visualization dynamically, such as selecting a specific time period or metric. Actions provide the ability to link multiple visualizations together, allowing users to interact with one visualization and affect others.
Tableau Charts | How & When To Use Different Tableau Charts | Edureka image widget
Visual Encodings
​​​​​​​Visualization algorithms refer to various techniques and methods used to convert data into visual representations. These algorithms are crucial for creating meaningful and informative visualizations from complex datasets.
Tableau Documentation: Shelves and Cards Reference

​
In Tableau, visual encodings refer to the mapping of data attributes to different visual elements in a visualization. Tableau provides a user-friendly interface that allows you to create various types of visualizations by selecting appropriate encodings for your data. Here are some common visual encodings available in Tableau:

  1. Color: You can map a data attribute to the color of the visual elements (such as bars, points, or lines) in your chart. Color encodings are useful for representing categorical or continuous data.

  2. Size: This encoding allows you to map a data attribute to the size of the visual elements. Larger elements represent higher values, and smaller elements represent lower values.

  3. Shape: With shape encoding, you can use different shapes (e.g., circles, squares, triangles) to represent categories in your data.

  4. Position (x and y axes): The most fundamental encoding in a Cartesian coordinate system. You can map data attributes to the positions of visual elements along the x and y axes.

  5. Label: You can display data labels directly on the visual elements to show specific values or labels associated with the data points.

  6. Detail: This encoding allows you to add additional information to your visualization. For example, you can add a tooltip to show more details about a data point when hovering over it.

  7. Tooltip: You can customize the tooltip to show specific data attributes when hovering over visual elements.

  8. Transparency (Alpha): Mapping data attributes to transparency levels allows you to create layered visualizations and highlight certain data points.

  9. Angle: Angle encoding is useful for circular charts like pie charts or radial bar charts, where data attributes are mapped to the angles of the visual elements.

  10. Density (Density Mark): Tableau offers density marks for visualizing the distribution and intensity of data points in a specific area.

When creating visualizations in Tableau, you can drag and drop data fields onto the appropriate shelves (Columns, Rows, Marks, Color, Size, etc.) to apply these visual encodings. Tableau automatically applies default visual encodings based on the data types, but you can customize and fine-tune them to better convey the insights from your data effectively.

Different Types of Charts
​​​​​​​Tableau offers a wide range of chart types to help you visualize and analyze your data effectively.

Some of the most commonly used chart types available in Tableau:

  1. Bar Chart: Displays data using horizontal or vertical bars, where the length of each bar represents the data value. It is suitable for comparing categorical data.
    Tableau Documentation: Build a Bar Chart

    ​

  2. Line Chart: Connects data points with straight lines, often used to show trends and changes over time or continuous data.
    Tableau Documentation: Build Line Charts

    ​

  3. Scatter Plot: Plots individual data points on a Cartesian plane, with one variable on the x-axis and another on the y-axis. It is useful for identifying relationships and patterns between two continuous variables.
    Tableau Documentation: Build a Scatter Plot​​​​​​

    ​

  4. Pie Chart: Represents proportions of different categories as slices of a circular chart. The size of each slice is proportional to the percentage it represents.
    Tableau Documentation: Build a Pie Chart

    ​

  5. Area Chart: Similar to line charts, but the area between the line and the x-axis is filled. It is useful for displaying cumulative data or stacked proportions.
    Tableau Documentation: Create an Area Chart

    ​

  6. Histogram: Displays the distribution of continuous data by dividing it into intervals (bins) and showing the frequency of data points within each bin.
    Tableau Documentation: Build a Histogram

    ​

  7. Heat Map: Uses color gradients to represent different values in a matrix. It is useful for visualizing relationships between two variables simultaneously.
    Tableau Documentation: Build a Highlight Table or Heatmap

    ​

  8. Treemap: Displays hierarchical data as nested rectangles, with each level of the hierarchy represented as a set of rectangles. The size of each rectangle is proportional to its value.
    Tableau Documentation: Build a Tree map

    ​

  9. Gantt Chart: Represents project timelines, with tasks shown on the y-axis and their duration displayed as horizontal bars.
    Tableau Documentation: Build a Gantt Chart

    ​

  10. Box Plot: Illustrates the distribution of data using quartiles, outliers, and the median. It is helpful for identifying the spread and skewness of data.
    Tableau Documentation: Build a Box Plot

    ​

  11. Bullet Graph: A variation of a bar chart that provides more context by displaying a target, actual, and comparative performance measures.
    Tableau Documentation: Build a Bullet Chart

    ​

  12. Waterfall Chart: Visualizes the cumulative effect of positive and negative changes to an initial value. It is often used for financial and profit analysis.

    ​

  13. Dual-Axis Chart: Combines two different chart types on the same axes to compare two measures with different scales.
    Tableau Documentation: Add Axes for Multiple Measures in Views

    ​

  14. Pareto Chart: Combines a bar chart with a line graph to show the cumulative distribution of a categorical variable in descending order.
    Tableau Documentation: Create a Pareto Chart

    ​

These are just a few examples of the many chart types available in Tableau. Each chart type is suited for different data scenarios and insights, and Tableau's intuitive interface allows you to easily create and customize visualizations according to your needs.

Create a Pareto Chart - Tableau image widget
Choosing Appropriate Visualization
​​​​​​​Choosing the appropriate visualization in Tableau depends on the type of data you have and the insights you want to communicate.
Tableau Documentation: Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data
Some general guidelines to help you select the right visualization:

  1. Bar Charts: Use bar charts to compare different categories or discrete data points. They are effective for displaying counts, sums, or averages. For example, comparing sales of different products or showing the number of customers in each region.

  2. Line Charts: Use line charts to show trends over time or continuous data. They are suitable for visualizing data with a clear sequence or order. For instance, tracking stock prices over a period or analyzing website traffic over time.

  3. Pie Charts: Pie charts are ideal for displaying the parts of a whole. However, they can be less effective when comparing multiple categories or showing precise values. Consider using bar charts instead when the data involves more than a few categories.

  4. Scatter Plots: Use scatter plots to explore relationships between two numerical variables. They are useful for identifying patterns, correlations, or clusters in the data.

  5. Heat Maps: Heat maps work well for showing the distribution of data across two dimensions, often using color intensity to represent values. They are great for visualizing large datasets and identifying patterns.

  6. Map Visualizations: If your data contains geographical information, map visualizations can be valuable for displaying regional trends and comparisons.

  7. Gantt Charts: Gantt charts are perfect for visualizing project timelines, showing tasks and their durations.

  8. Histograms: Histograms are suitable for understanding the distribution of a single numerical variable. They display the frequency of data within predefined bins.

  9. Box Plots: Use box plots to understand the distribution of data and identify outliers. They are particularly useful for comparing distributions across different categories.

  10. Tree Maps: Tree maps are useful when you want to visualize hierarchical data, such as market share of different products within various product categories.
